title: Buying weaners and settling them in
subtitle: Sourcing, transporting, and settling two to three outdoor weaners on their first day and first week.
excerpt: A weaner arrives on your holding at six to eight weeks old weighing around 18 to 25 kg. The first 48 hours are the period of highest stress and the most common time for escape or injury. Getting the electric fence working, the shelter bedded, water available, and food routine established before the pigs arrive removes almost every avoidable first-week problem.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Outdoor Pig Production guide. https://ahdb.org.uk

content.text: , register a County Parish Holding (CPH) number with the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA). You cannot legally receive pigs without one. The registration is free and takes a few days; apply online via the Rural Payments Agency portal. The vendor will need your CPH number to raise the
movement licence before the pigs leave their holding.
Before collection: what to have ready

The arc should be in position and bedded with dry straw. The electric fence should be set up, earthed, tested, and running hot. Water should be in place. Have feed on site before you collect. Arriving home with pigs and nothing for them to do except test the fence immediately is the most common first-day mistake.
Back the trailer to the paddock gate and open it. Let the pigs walk out in their own time. Never drag a pig by a leg. Use a
to guide them toward the arc if they scatter. Pigs are curious and will explore the whole paddock in the first hour. This is normal.
of six days applies to your holding. No other pigs may leave during this period. Keep the arrival date in your pig register: the standstill is a legal requirement, not a recommendation.

attrs.body: You may feed pigs proprietary pig pellets, vegetables grown on your own land, and some milling co-products. You may not feed catering waste, kitchen scraps (including vegetable peelings that have been in a kitchen), or any material that has been in contact with meat. This is the swill-feeding ban under the Animal Feed (England) Regulations 2010. The rule exists because the 2001 UK foot-and-mouth outbreak is traceable to illegally fed catering waste.
attrs.title: Feeding rules
